old_claim.note: Riveting Technology provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct riveting_technology dependency remains because the suspension_bridges node is dated to early suspension-bridge construction, while the corrected riveting node is anchored to 1860s hydraulic industrial riveting.

source_shape.source_locator: Construction and history sections describe suspension bridges through towers, caissons, anchorages, eyebars, spun wire cables, and late-18th/early-19th-century development, without making riveting a direct enabling feature.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Britannica grounds suspension-bridge construction in cable, tower, anchorage, and deck mechanics rather than in industrial riveting.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Because the corrected riveting node is 1860s hydraulic platework, keeping it as a direct prerequisite for an 1826 suspension-bridge node would introduce an unsupported and chronologically later dependency.

ontology_before: Steel-Cable Suspension Bridges depended directly on Riveting Technology, laundering a later industrial plate-joining technology into the early suspension-bridge scope.
ontology_after: Steel-Cable Suspension Bridges keep cable, construction, mathematics, steel, and production context without depending directly on the later hydraulic riveting node.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: the direct riveting_technology dependency is removed from suspension_bridges.
would_reject_if: The suspension_bridges node is later narrowed to a post-1860 riveted-plate bridge form.
A source shows the 1826 suspension-bridge claim directly required industrial riveting technology.
The graph adds a separate bridge-deck-fabrication node that should explicitly require riveting.
short_reason: The corrected riveting node is later hydraulic platework, not a direct prerequisite for the current suspension-bridge scope.
